title: Open Channel Flow Hydraulics
description: A pipe with an inside diameter of 18.8 inches contains water to a depth of 15.7 inches. The pipe is on a 1.5% slope and has a Manning’s n of 0.012. What is the hydraulic radius in inches?
hint: Use Manning's Equation: Q = (1.49/n) * A * R^(2/3) * S^(1/2). Remember Hydraulic Radius R = Area / Wetted Perimeter.
shortcut: For wide rectangular channels, Hydraulic Radius (R) is approximately equal to the depth (y). This saves you from calculating the full wetted perimeter!
fifthGrader: Imagine sliding down a water slide. If the slide is smooth, you go fast. If it's sticky, you go slow. We're just calculating how much water can slide down a pipe based on how sticky the walls are!

title: Concrete Canal Lining
description: A 227-ft length of canal is to be lined with concrete for erosion control. With 12% allowance for waste and overexcavation, the volume (yd³) of concrete that must be delivered is most nearly:
hint: Find the maximum bending moment (M = wL^2/8 for a simply supported uniform load). Then check ACI or AISC tables for capacity.
shortcut: For reinforced concrete, if the steel yields before the concrete crushes, it's a tension-controlled (ductile) failure, which is exactly what you want.
fifthGrader: If you hold a ruler at both ends and push down in the middle, it bends. We are just doing math to find out exactly how much weight it takes to snap the ruler in half.

title: Track Loader Depreciation
description: A track loader has an initial cost of $75,000 and a salvage value of $10,000 at the end of its expected life of 10 years. Based on the straight-line method of depreciation, at the end of the track loader's 8th year, its book value is most nearly:
hint: Draw a cash flow diagram! Use the Engineering Economics tables in the NCEES handbook. Pay attention to Present Worth (P) vs Annual (A) costs.
shortcut: For capitalized cost (infinite life), the formula is just Present Worth = Annual Cost / Interest Rate (P = A/i).
fifthGrader: If you buy a car today, it's worth less money every year because it gets old. We are just using math to figure out exactly how much money the car will be worth in 10 years.

hint: Apply the Energy Equation (Bernoulli) or Hazen-Williams for friction loss. Don't forget to account for minor losses (valves/bends) if given K values.

title: Wick Drain Effluent Disposal Cost
description: Site preparation and grading require the placement of 20 ft of new fill. An analysis of the resulting consolidation of the underlying soft, saturated, compressible deposits reveals a mean consolidation settlement of 22 in. affecting a 21.5-acre area. Prefabricated wick drains will be used to accelerate the settlement to meet the project schedule. Because of contamination from the former site use, the effluent from the wick drains must be collected and treated prior to disposal.
hint: Check phase diagrams (Weight vs Volume). For consolidation, use the Compression Index (Cc) formula: Sc = [Cc * H / (1+e0)] * log(P2/P1).
shortcut: If a soil is fully saturated, the Volume of Voids equals the Volume of Water. You can skip a bunch of math steps if S = 100%!
fifthGrader: Think of soil like a sponge full of water. If you put a heavy brick on the sponge, the water slowly squishes out and the sponge gets flatter. We are measuring how flat it gets!

title: Detention Basin Fill Time
description: A stormwater drainage ditch with a maximum capacity of 10 cfs discharges into a detention basin. The detention basin volume is 400,000 gal. During a storm event, the average discharge into the detention basin was 1.5 cfs. The time (hours) to fill the empty basin is most nearly:
hint: Use the Rational Method (Q = CiA) for small watersheds. Ensure Area is in acres and Intensity is in inches/hour.
shortcut: In the Rational Method (Q=CiA), the units magically work out so that 1 acre-inch/hour is almost exactly equal to 1 cubic foot per second (cfs). No conversion needed!
fifthGrader: When it rains on a parking lot, the water has nowhere to soak in, so it all runs into the storm drain. We are calculating how big the drain needs to be so the parking lot doesn't flood!

hint: Apply the Energy Equation (Bernoulli) or Hazen-Williams for friction loss. Don't forget to account for minor losses (valves/bends) if given K values.
shortcut: Velocity head (v^2/2g) is often negligibly small compared to pressure and elevation heads. You can usually ignore it for a quick first estimate.
fifthGrader: When you blow water through a straw, it's harder if the straw is super long or really skinny. We are just measuring how much energy the water loses while squeezing through the pipe.

description: A horizontal confined aquifer is made up of three layers with the following properties: me [TE] fa Lower 210 03 | ‘The aquifer transmissivity (*/day) is most nearly: Fe er oe SE “ oe
hint: Use the Thiem equation for steady state or Theis equation for transient. Transmissivity (T) = Hydraulic Conductivity (K) * Aquifer Thickness (b).
shortcut: If Transmissivity is very high, drawdown will be very low. If the multiple choice answers show a massive drop in water level for a gravel aquifer, eliminate them.
fifthGrader: Imagine an underground sponge full of water. If you stick a straw in and suck really fast, the water around the straw dips down like a funnel. We are measuring that dip!

title: Problem 66
description: The primary clarifier of a trickling filter plant receives 1,000 Ib of solids daily. The clarifier has a. solid capture at of 0% and produces an underflow sludge concentration of 9% The volume of primary sludge (ft'/day) is most nearly: oA 2 ob % oc 10 oD 210
hint: Use the mass flux equation: Mass (lb/day) = Flow (MGD) * Concentration (mg/L) * 8.34.
shortcut: The number 8.34 is magic! It's the weight of 1 gallon of water in pounds. Always use it when converting MGD and mg/L to pounds.
fifthGrader: Imagine your city's wastewater is a giant river filled with tiny food particles. We are calculating how many pounds of particles we caught in our net before the water went into the lake.

title: Culvert Design Flow
description: A 48-inch diameter concrete culvert (n=0.013) is designed to carry a peak flow of 120 cfs under inlet control. Assuming the entrance loss coefficient Ke is 0.5, the required headwater depth (ft) is most nearly:
hint: For inlet control, use the FHWA nomograph or the specific inlet control equations based on Q/(AD^0.5).
shortcut: If it's inlet control, the barrel slope and length don't matter—only the inlet geometry and headwater.
fifthGrader: Think of a funnel. If you pour water in too fast, it backs up. We are just measuring how high the water backs up before the pipe can swallow it all!

title: BOD5 Removal Efficiency
description: A primary clarifier receives a flow of 2.5 MGD with an influent BOD5 of 220 mg/L. If the effluent BOD5 is 140 mg/L, the daily mass of BOD5 removed (lb/day) is most nearly:
hint: Mass (lb/day) = Flow (MGD) × Concentration (mg/L) × 8.34 lb/gal.
shortcut: Calculate the difference in concentration first (220 - 140 = 80 mg/L), then multiply by flow and 8.34.
fifthGrader: If you have 220 pieces of trash in the water, and 140 make it out, you caught 80 pieces! Multiply that by how much water flows each day to find total trash caught.

title: Retaining Wall Overturning Moment
description: A 15-ft high cantilever retaining wall holds back backfill with a unit weight of 120 pcf and an active earth pressure coefficient (Ka) of 0.33. The overturning moment (lb-ft/ft) about the toe is most nearly:
hint: Active force Pa = 0.5 * Ka * gamma * H^2. The moment arm is H/3.
shortcut: Since the triangular load acts at 1/3 the height from the base, overturning moment = Pa * (15/3).
fifthGrader: Pushing a tall bookshelf is easiest at the top! The dirt pushes the wall in a triangle shape, so we measure the total push and multiply by how high up it acts.

title: Horizontal Curve Sight Distance
description: A horizontal curve has a radius of 1,200 ft. A retaining wall is built on the inside of the curve. The minimum clearance distance (Middle Ordinate, M) required to provide a stopping sight distance of 400 ft is most nearly:
hint: Use the formula: M = R [1 - cos(28.65 * S / R)], where S is sight distance and R is radius.
shortcut: For quick approximation, M ≈ S² / (8R).
fifthGrader: If you're driving around a sharp turn and there's a wall on the inside, you can't see around it! This math tells us exactly how far back the wall needs to be so you don't crash.

title: Capitalized Cost Analysis
description: A new pump station has a first cost of $150,000, annual maintenance of $5,000, and must be replaced every 20 years at a cost of $120,000. If the interest rate is 6%, the capitalized cost is most nearly:
hint: Capitalized Cost (CC) = First Cost + P(A/i) + P(A/F, i, n). Convert all recurring costs to their Present Worth over an infinite life.
shortcut: Capitalized cost just means 'Present Worth of a project that lasts forever'. Use CC = P + (A/i).
fifthGrader: If you wanted to put enough money in a magic bank account today so the interest pays for this pump station forever and ever, how much money do you need? That's Capitalized Cost!

title: Driveway Grading & FFE
description: Determine the required Finished Floor Elevation (FFE) of the main house and garage, distance from the road, and road centerline elevation to ensure positive drainage (rain will not enter the garage). The garage slab is exactly 0.55 ft below the FFE of the house.
hint: Sketch a profile view. Start at the road centerline, subtract the cross slope to the curb, add the curb height, and then calculate the rise along the driveway using its slope and length.
shortcut: Elevation = Starting Elevation + (Slope * Distance). Make sure your slopes are converted from percentages to decimals (e.g., 2% = 0.02).
fifthGrader: Think of this like building a ramp for your toy cars. If the road is the bottom of the ramp, we need to make sure the driveway goes UP to the garage so that water rolls down to the street, not into the house!

title: Driveway Runoff Energy
description: Using the Energy Equation, determine how far horizontally stormwater runoff from the road will travel up a driveway. Evaluate whether the initial kinetic energy of the water will overcome the driveway slope and cause the runoff to enter the garage.
hint: Use Conservation of Energy: Kinetic Energy (Velocity Head) converts to Potential Energy (Elevation Head). V^2 / 2g = Elevation change.
shortcut: Max Distance = (V^2 / 2g) / Driveway Slope.
fifthGrader: Think of rolling a ball up a hill. If you roll it really fast (high velocity), it rolls further up the hill before stopping and rolling back down. If the hill is steep, it stops sooner!
